players.name: Mathew Ryan
players.bio: Australia's most-capped goalkeeper with over 100 appearances, Mathew Ryan is the Socceroos' last line of defence. A commanding shot-stopper who's played across Europe's top leagues, he's a veteran of three World Cups.
players.facts: Ryan once saved a penalty in the 2023 Asian Cup — at 32, he's still the man Australians trust most between the sticks.

players.name: Miloš Degenek
players.bio: Born in the Serbian Krajina and raised in Germany, Degenek embodies the multicultural fabric of Australian football. A rugged, experienced centre-back, he's been a dependable figure in defence across stints in Japan, the US, and Europe.
players.facts: Degenek has played club football on four continents — Europe, Asia, North America, and more. A true football nomad.

players.name: Alessandro Circati
players.bio: Born in Fidenza, Italy, Circati chose the Socceroos over the Azzurri — and at just 22, he's already made his mark. The Parma centre-back is composed on the ball and fierce in the air, a rare prospect for Australian football.
players.facts: Circati already has an international goal at 22 — pretty handy for a centre-back who nearly played for Italy.

players.name: Jacob Italiano
players.bio: Perth-born right-back Jacob Italiano has made the long journey from Western Australia to Austria's Grazer AK. Still relatively new to international football with just 5 caps, he's a determined, attack-minded full-back hungry for more.
players.facts: With the surname Italiano and playing in Austria for an Austrian club, Jacob keeps everyone guessing his football heritage.

players.name: Jordan Bos
players.bio: Melbourne-born but Rotterdam-developed, Jordan Bos is one of the most exciting young full-backs in the Socceroos squad. Playing for Feyenoord, he brings genuine European pedigree and a goal threat — four international goals at just 23.
players.facts: Bos plays his club football at the same Feyenoord club that produced Robin van Persie. Not bad company to keep.

players.name: Jason Geria
players.bio: Canberra-born Jason Geria has carved out a journeyman career that's taken him to Japan's J-League with Albirex Niigata. A tenacious, hardworking right-back, he brings experience and defensive solidity to the Socceroos squad at 33.
players.facts: Geria represents the ACT in a squad full of Melburnians — Canberra's lone ranger on the World Cup stage.

players.name: Mathew Leckie
players.bio: A Socceroos legend, Leckie's iconic solo goal against Denmark at Qatar 2022 sent Australia into the Round of 16. Now 35 and back home at Melbourne City, the veteran winger brings pace, passion, and priceless big-game experience to his third World Cup.
players.facts: Leckie's 2022 World Cup solo goal vs Denmark is arguably the greatest in Socceroos history. He scored it aged 31.

players.name: Connor Metcalfe
players.bio: A combative, progressive midfielder from Newcastle, NSW, Metcalfe has thrived in the Bundesliga 2 with FC St. Pauli. He covers ground relentlessly and isn't afraid to get stuck in — the engine room type every midfield needs.
players.facts: St. Pauli are famous for their punk rock, anti-fascist fan culture — Metcalfe fits right into football's most rebellious club.

players.name: Mohamed Touré
players.bio: Born in Conakry, Guinea, Touré chose Australia and has wasted no time making an impression — two goals in just 10 caps at age 22. The Norwich City striker is powerful, direct, and could be Australia's long-term No.9 solution.
players.facts: Touré was born in Guinea, grew up in Australia, and now plays in England. He's taken quite the scenic route to the World Cup.

players.name: Ajdin Hrustic
players.bio: Melbourne-born with Bosnian roots, Hrustic is the magician in Australia's midfield — a cultured playmaker with an eye for a pass and a deadly set-piece delivery. He's worn the No.10 shirt with pride and creativity.
players.facts: Hrustic once played for the Netherlands youth teams before switching allegiance to Australia. Their loss, our gain.

players.name: Awer Mabil
players.bio: One of football's most inspiring stories: born in a Kenyan refugee camp, Mabil fled war-torn South Sudan before finding his calling on a football pitch. Now a pacy, skilful winger with 10 international goals, he's a hero on and off the field.
players.facts: Mabil learned to play football barefoot in Kakuma refugee camp. He's never forgotten his roots, advocating for refugees globally.

players.name: Paul Izzo
players.bio: Adelaide-born Paul Izzo is the squad's experienced backup goalkeeper, currently plying his trade in Denmark with Randers. Reliable and professional, he's the calm presence ensuring Ryan has quality competition for the gloves.
players.facts: Izzo shares a surname with no one famous in football — but he's quietly become one of the more travelled Aussie keepers around.

players.name: Aiden O'Neill
players.bio: Brisbane-born O'Neill is a disciplined, intelligent defensive midfielder who reads the game well. Now in MLS with New York City FC, he brings structure and tenacity to the Socceroos' midfield, making him a trusted option for the coach.
players.facts: O'Neill was born on 4 July — American Independence Day. Coincidentally, he now plays in New York. The universe planned this.

players.name: Cammy Devlin
players.bio: Sydney-born but heart-and-soul Scottish Premiership, Cammy Devlin has become a cult hero at Hearts with his lung-busting runs and tireless work rate. He brings that same relentless energy to the Socceroos' midfield battle.
players.facts: Devlin is beloved by Hearts fans in Edinburgh — the Sydney boy who became a Scottish football cult hero. Remarkable crossover.

players.name: Kai Trewin
players.bio: From the small NSW coastal town of Batemans Bay to New York City FC — Trewin's journey is the stuff of dreams. A composed, intelligent defensive midfielder, the 25-year-old is building his Socceroos career steadily with 6 caps so far.
players.birth_location: Batemans Bay, New South Wales, Australia
players.facts: Trewin grew up in Batemans Bay, population ~12,000. It's produced exactly one FIFA World Cup player — him.

players.name: Aziz Behich
players.bio: One of Australian football's most consistent servants, Behich has been marauding down the left flank for over a decade. Now 35, the Melbourne City stalwart brings vast experience and a never-say-die attitude to his likely final World Cup.
players.facts: Behich has represented Australia at three World Cups. He's seen it all — and still runs like it's his first cap.

players.name: Nestory Irankunda
players.bio: Born in Tanzania, raised in Australia, and dazzling at Watford — Irankunda is the most exciting teenager in Australian football. Explosive pace, silky footwork, and 5 goals in 15 caps at just 20 make him the tournament's wildcard to watch.
players.facts: Irankunda was born in 2006 — meaning he was still in primary school when Australia reached the 2022 World Cup quarter-finals.

players.name: Patrick Beach
players.bio: Sydney-born Patrick Beach is Australia's youngest goalkeeper in the squad and one of the most promising keepers in the A-League with Melbourne City. Only 22 with 2 caps, this is his World Cup education — and he's paying close attention.
players.facts: Beach and Ryan share the same squad — one is 34 with 104 caps, the other 22 with 2. The changing of the guard is coming.

players.name: Harry Souttar
players.bio: Aberdeen-born but proudly Australian, Souttar is a giant centre-back who scores like a striker — 11 international goals from defence is extraordinary. The Leicester City man is a colossus in the air and a genuine set-piece weapon.
players.facts: Souttar has 11 international goals as a centre-back. For context, that's more than many forwards ever score for Australia.

players.name: Cristian Volpato
players.bio: A technically gifted attacking midfielder born in Camperdown, NSW, Volpato caught the eye at Roma's academy before joining Sassuolo. Still only 22 with just 1 cap, this World Cup could be the stage where the world finally notices him.
players.facts: Volpato chose Australia over Italy — Roma's youth system developed him, but the Socceroos get to enjoy the finished product.

players.name: Cameron Burgess
players.bio: Another Aberdonian in the Socceroos squad, Cameron Burgess has been a solid performer for Swansea City in the Championship. A no-nonsense centre-back with 27 caps, he provides reliable depth and leadership in Australia's defensive unit.
players.facts: Burgess and Souttar are both from Aberdeen — two Scots who ended up playing for Australia. The tartan Socceroos connection is real.

players.name: Jackson Irvine
players.bio: Melbourne-born Jackson Irvine is the heartbeat of the Socceroos midfield — a box-to-box workhorse with a surprisingly sharp eye for goal. With 82 caps and 14 goals, he leads from the front and often from the middle of the pitch.
players.facts: Irvine and Leckie both play for FC St. Pauli — two of Australia's most experienced players sharing a dressing room. Efficient.

players.name: Nishan Velupillay
players.full_name: Nishan Matthew Velupillay; நிஷான் மேத்யூ வேலுப்பிள்ளை
players.bio: Melbourne-born Velupillay is a tricky, unpredictable left winger who has lit up the A-League with Melbourne Victory. Three goals in just 7 caps suggests he has a knack for the big moment — and the World Cup stage won't faze him.
players.facts: Velupillay's Tamil-Australian heritage makes him one of the most culturally unique players at the 2026 World Cup.

players.name: Paul Okon-Engstler
players.bio: Born in Ghent, Belgium — the son of former Socceroos legend Paul Okon — Okon-Engstler has football in his DNA. The 21-year-old Sydney FC midfielder is carving his own legacy, following dad's footsteps onto the World Cup stage.
players.facts: His father Paul Okon played at the 1994 and 1998 World Cups for Australia. Now the son completes the family double.

players.name: Lucas Herrington
players.bio: At just 18, Brisbane-born Lucas Herrington is the youngest player in Australia's World Cup squad — and one of the youngest in the entire tournament. The Colorado Rapids centre-back is a remarkable talent getting the ultimate crash course in elite football.
players.facts: Herrington was born in 2007 — the year the iPhone launched. He's barely old enough to drive, yet he's at a World Cup.

players.name: Tete Yengi
players.bio: Adelaide-born Tete Yengi made the most of his debut — scoring on his first international appearance. Now at Machida Zelvia in Japan, the 25-year-old striker is a powerful, clinical forward who could prove a useful impact substitute.
players.facts: Yengi scored on his first ever Socceroos appearance. That's the dream debut — one cap, one goal, one World Cup ticket.
